reasoning_model_1: The model accurately reproduces the text content without errors. Regarding the core paralinguistic requirement, the audio successfully starts with a low pitch, adhering to the first part of the instruction. However, the requested 'gradual transition to a medium pitch' is not effectively realized; the voice tends to remain in the lower register throughout the clip rather than rising noticeably to a medium range. Furthermore, the speaking pace is unnaturally slow (spanning roughly 12 seconds for a short sentence), which negatively impacts the fluency and makes the delivery sound somewhat labored and mechanical.
reasoning_model_2: The model generates the text accurately with high fluency and a natural speaking rate (approximately 7 seconds). However, it fails completely on the core paralinguistic instruction. The audio does not start with a 'very low pitch' but rather begins and continues in a standard, medium pitch range. There is no audible attempt to perform the specific dynamic adjustment (Low to Medium ramp) requested by the user. It effectively functions as a standard text-to-speech generation that ignores the style prompt.
model_comparison: Model 1 is the winner. While Model 1 suffers from unnatural pacing (too slow) and fails to complete the full transition (staying low rather than rising), it successfully modified its voice to meet the 'very low pitch' starting constraint. Model 2, despite being more fluent and natural, completely ignored the paralinguistic instructions, producing a standard output with no pitch adjustment. In the context of evaluating 'Dynamic Adjustment' capabilities, Model 1 demonstrates partial control, whereas Model 2 demonstrates none.
